critic_raw:
This CNN-RNN versus logistic regression design is well-specified in many respects, but several structural problems undermine the comparability of the comparison.

First, the CNN-RNN model processes temporal sequences of treatments (procedures, medications) using convolutional layers for local pattern extraction followed by LSTM for temporal dependencies, then concatenates with static features. The logistic regression baseline receives 'all engineered features' but explicitly excludes 'raw sequences, which logistic regression cannot process natively.' This creates a meaningful feature asymmetry: the CNN-RNN has access to the full sequence of treatment events, while logistic regression only receives aggregated summaries. The design justifies this as 'part of the hypothesis,' but this framing conflates architecture comparison with feature comparison. A gradient-boosted tree or nonlinear SVM trained on the same engineered features as the logistic regression baseline would establish whether the gap is due to sequencing capacity or nonlinear expressiveness — neither is tested here.

Second, the department-level readmission baseline (population mean from training data) is included as a feature for both models. This feature is a computed statistic derived from training labels and injected directly into the feature set. For the logistic regression baseline, this is essentially including the training set's department-specific label prior as a feature — a form of target statistics encoding. If the department-level baseline is computed on all training data (including the patient being scored), this is a direct label leakage. The design says it is 'computed from training data only,' which prevents leakage to test, but does not address whether it is computed with the current record excluded (leave-one-out encoding), which would be required for correct target statistics.

Third, the CNN-RNN architecture specification is given with fixed hyperparameters ('2 CNN blocks, 32 and 64 filters, kernel size 3, 1 LSTM layer 64 units, 2 dense layers 128 and 32 units') but the design also states hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set. This is contradictory — either the architecture is fixed a priori or it is tuned. If the stated architecture is just the search space center, this should be made explicit. If it is a fixed specification, the logistic regression receives tuning while the neural model does not, which reverses the 'equivalent tuning effort' claim.

Fourth, AUPRC is selected as the primary metric because of class imbalance (~15-20% positive rate). At 15-20% positive rate, the imbalance is relatively mild; AUPRC advantage over AUROC is most pronounced at extreme imbalance (e.g., <5%). With moderate imbalance, AUROC and AUPRC tend to be concordant, so this metric choice has minimal practical consequence for interpretation, but the stated justification overreaches.
all_issues_raised: The CNN-RNN has access to full treatment sequences while the logistic regression baseline only receives aggregated features; this is a feature scope difference masquerading as an architecture comparison, and without a nonlinear model (e.g., gradient boosting) trained on identical features, it is impossible to attribute any gap to sequential modeling capacity versus nonlinear expressiveness.

The department-level readmission baseline feature (mean readmission rate from training data) constitutes target statistics encoding; without leave-one-out or out-of-fold computation, this feature may leak partial label information into training examples, inflating both models' performance in a way that differentially benefits the logistic regression if it relies more heavily on this feature.

The model architecture is specified with fixed hyperparameters (specific filter counts, kernel sizes, LSTM units) while simultaneously claiming hyperparameter tuning occurs on validation data — these are contradictory; if the stated architecture is fixed, the logistic regression receives tuning effort the neural model does not.

AUPRC is selected as the primary metric on the grounds of class imbalance, but at 15-20% positive rate the imbalance is mild enough that AUROC and AUPRC are typically concordant; the metric justification is overstated, and if the two metrics diverge substantially the design provides no guidance on which to trust.
all_issues_adjudicated: The CNN-RNN has access to full treatment sequences while the logistic regression baseline only receives aggregated features; this is a feature scope difference masquerading as an architecture comparison, and without a nonlinear model (e.g., gradient boosting) trained on identical features, it is impossible to attribute any gap to sequential modeling capacity versus nonlinear expressiveness.

The department-level readmission baseline feature (mean readmission rate from training data) constitutes target statistics encoding; without leave-one-out or out-of-fold computation, this feature may leak partial label information into training examples, inflating both models' performance in a way that differentially benefits the logistic regression if it relies more heavily on this feature.

The model architecture is specified with fixed hyperparameters (specific filter counts, kernel sizes, LSTM units) while simultaneously claiming hyperparameter tuning occurs on validation data — these are contradictory; if the stated architecture is fixed, the logistic regression receives tuning effort the neural model does not.

AUPRC is selected as the primary metric on the grounds of class imbalance, but at 15-20% positive rate the imbalance is mild enough that AUROC and AUPRC are typically concordant; the metric justification is overstated, and if the two metrics diverge substantially the design provides no guidance on which to trust.
